dialogues.text: You've practiced equations with all kinds of groups and unknowns. Let's connect what you discovered.
Four equations. Three are true — both sides have the same value. One is false. Which one is false?
3 times 5 is 15, not 20. The sides don't match.
And notice — the other equations are all true. The total is on the right in A and on the left in B and D, but both sides have the same value either way. That's what the equals sign checks.
Three pictures. One equation fits all of them. What makes them the same?
Both true! They all have 8 things — and the reason is the same every time: 4 groups of 2. The structure underneath is what the equation captures.
Three equations. Same numbers: 4, 5, and 20. But the unknown moved to a different spot each time. What stays the same?
4 groups of 5 equals 20. That relationship stays the same no matter where the unknown is.
The unknown moves, but the relationship stays — 4 groups of 5 is always 20. You figure out whichever piece is missing.
Here's an equation you have seen before. And here's one from today. What changed?
All of these are different today. Three new things, all in one equation. The equals sign works both ways. Unknowns can go anywhere. And equations work for stacks, rows, groups — not just bags.
If you had to remember one thing from today, what would it be?
Same value as—that's the equals sign's job. You'll use that in every equation from now on.
Unknowns move, but the relationship stays. You can figure out any missing piece.
Rows, stacks, groups—different pictures, same math underneath.

You came in knowing expressions and products. Now you write complete equations. You know the equals sign means same value as. You find unknowns in any position. And you see equal groups everywhere: bags, rows, stacks, groups.
Next time, you'll see equal groups arranged in a new way.
Rows AND columns. You'll have a lot to notice.
